questions.scenario: The production subscription cost is 32 percent higher than the previous 30 days. The first view is only a monthly total. What is the best next slice?

options.text: Group by service name, then filter to the DevPolaris resource groups and inspect the daily trend.
Resize the largest-looking resource before checking which service family changed.
Delete all staging resources because staging is always the cause of cost increases.
Wait until the end of the next month because Cost Analysis cannot answer service-level questions.

questions.explanation: The article starts with visibility. Grouping and filtering turn a total into service, resource group, resource, tag, and time evidence before any tuning decision.

questions.scenario:
Cost Analysis shows this change:

```text
Service: Azure Monitor
Resource: log-devpolaris-prod
Daily pattern: flat until release 2026-05-16, then steady increase
Traffic: normal
```

What should the team investigate first?

options.text: Log ingestion, retention, and application logging behavior near the release.
SQL backup retention, because Azure Monitor cost is always database backup cost.
Only the subscription display name, because service and time evidence are not useful.
A complete migration to another region before reading log volume.

questions.explanation: The service and time trend point at observability volume. The next useful question is what logging or retention behavior changed near the release.

questions.scenario: Which Cost Analysis dimensions help turn spend into owned evidence?

options.text: Service name, because it separates compute, SQL, storage, monitoring, networking, and backup cost.
Resource group, because it can show the service or environment boundary where spend changed.
Tag values such as `service`, `env`, and `owner`, when they are present and reliable.
The prettiest resource name, because naming style proves the resource is safe to resize.

questions.explanation: Service, resource group, and tag dimensions help identify what changed and who should review it. A friendly name can help humans, but it is not cost or safety evidence by itself.

questions.scenario: The team finds that deleted test databases still have short-term backup cost aging out. What is the safest conclusion?

options.text: Cost can remain after deletion until retention behavior finishes, so repeated create/delete cycles still need review.
Azure SQL backups prove the databases were never deleted.
All Azure SQL backup cost is a billing error and should be ignored.
The only fix is to remove every production backup immediately.

questions.explanation: Cost visibility should explain behavior, not trigger blind deletion. Retention can leave real cost after database churn, and production backup protection needs a separate recovery decision.
